retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: In refractory status epilepticus, a Cochrane Review found that thiopental was equally effective as propofol for controlling seizure activity. [3]  A systematic review and meta-analysis evaluated interventions for benzodiazepine-resistant status epilepticus in both children and adults. Among the studied interventions, phenobarbital and high-dose levetiracetam were superior to phenytoin in achieving seizure cessation within 60 minutes. Phenobarbital is effective, but concerns about its safety profile exist, including cardiovascular instability. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the appropriate indications for barbiturate use in various clinical scenarios, such as seizure disorders, neonatal withdrawal, insomnia, and induction of anesthesia. Implement safe and evidence-based practices when administering barbiturates, including proper dosing, monitoring, and adjusting for individual patient needs. Assess patients regularly for signs of adverse effects and monitor therapeutic efficacy, adapting treatment plans as necessary. Collaborate with the interprofessional healthcare team, including primary care, emergency medicine, and specialists, to develop comprehensive management plans for patients using barbiturates. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturate Toxicity -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Development of abuse-deterrent drug formulations and the use of safer drug packaging (eg, blister packs, child-resistant caps, and cautionary labeling) have also been employed to deter misuse. [40] Finally, educational campaigns aimed at prescribers, public health advisories, and evidence-based clinical guidelines have assisted in increasing knowledge regarding the dangers of barbiturate drugs and safer treatment alternatives. [41] [42]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Pediatric patients: In the pediatric population, the therapeutic approach for refractory and super-refractory status epilepticus (RSE/SRSE) involves using barbiturates such as phenobarbitone and thiopental for coma induction. These drugs act on GABA receptors, inhibiting N -methyl-D-aspartate (NMDA) receptors. While effective in controlling seizures, unique considerations exist in the pediatric population. Barbiturates, including thiopental, may have a prolonged recovery time and have adverse effects such as hypotension and respiratory depression, which may have specific implications in pediatric patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Phenobarbital: Phenobarbitone has extensive use as an antiepileptic drug in the neonatal and pediatric population. It is the most cost-effective drug treatment for epilepsy in adults in low-resource countries. [5] IV barbiturates have been used for neurosurgery due to reduced cerebral metabolic rate of oxygen consumption. [6] For individuals with severe traumatic brain injury (TBI), high-dose barbiturates may be a consideration but are not indicated for prophylactic administration. [7] In a review of practice in 5 European countries, about 20% of patients received barbiturates. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Methohexital: Methohexital has demonstrated safety and efficacy for procedural sedation of short duration for cardioversion and pediatric outpatient surgery. [10] [11] Methohexital can be used for fracture reduction in the emergency department and sedation for elective intubation in neonates. [12] [13] Methohexital has preferential use in electroconvulsive therapy due to longer seizure duration. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturate Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: With appropriate supportive care alone, the mortality rate of barbiturate toxicity is low. However, select patients may benefit from treatment strategies to enhance barbiturate elimination. Current recommendations for enhanced elimination are based on the pharmacokinetic and toxicokinetic properties of each barbiturate drug, clinical trials, case reports, and expert opinion. Adequately controlled trials supporting these strategies are lacking, however. [35] [3] Thus, enhanced elimination is only recommended for barbiturate toxicity with coma, need for respiratory support, or need for cardiovascular support (ie, when clinical benefits are likely to outweigh the risks of treatment). [35]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pain Assessment And Management -- Issues of Concern -- Pain Management
retrieved_docs.content: Diazepam Kinetics/dynamics Pregnancy category: D Half-life: 20 to 70 hours (active metabolite) Onset: 1 to 5 minutes (IV / IM) Duration: 15 to 60 minutes Dosing Adult Alcohol withdrawal IV: 10 mg initially followed by 5 to 10 mg every 1 to 4 hours Muscle relaxant IV/IM: 5 to 10 mg every 3 to 4 hours Seizure disorder / status epilepticus IV/IM: 5 to 10 mg/dose every 10 to 15 minutes Pediatric Muscle relaxant IV/IM: 0.04 to 0.2 mg/kg every 2 to 4 hours Seizure disorder / status epilepticus Neonate (< 28 days) IV: 0.3 to 0.75 mg/kg every 15 to 30 minutes x 2 to 3 doses > 1 month IV: 0.2 to 0.5 mg/kg every 15 to 30 minutes x 2 to 3 doses Precautions Not recommended for medication-assisted intubation (MAI) / rapid sequence intubation (RSI) because of the time of onset but frequently used for long-term sedation following intubation. It requires propylene glycol as a diluent, and there are reports of propylene glycol toxicity associated with long-term infusions. Undergoes hepatic oxidation and glucuronidation if metabolized

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturate Toxicity -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: In the 1970s, newer drugs with safer profiles (eg, benzodiazepines) began replacing barbiturates for many indications. At the same time, many sought to confront the dangers of barbiturates. Programs such as the Campaign for the Use and Restriction of Barbiturates advocated for awareness about the dangers of barbiturates and sponsored efforts to decrease prescriptions. Legislation like the Medicines Act of 1968 and The Controlled Substance Act also facilitated increased oversight and tighter control of barbiturates. [2] Both prescription rates of barbiturates and related poisonings decreased in the late 1980s. [7] By 2002, the American Association of Poison Control Centers noted only 375 cases and 21 deaths from barbiturate toxicity. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Breastfeeding considerations: Clinical data on the effects of phenobarbital indicate that inter and intrapatient variability of excretion in breast milk is apparent. Phenobarbital can cause drowsiness in infants, especially when used with other sedating drugs. In a series of breastfeeding infants, for each mg/kg of phenobarbital taken by mothers, the infant's serum concentration increased by 2 to 5 mg/L. Several case reports exist of infant sedation occurring in mothers treated with phenobarbital. Consequently, the infant requires monitoring drowsiness and weight gain, especially in younger, exclusively breastfed infants. If excessive sedation ensues, discontinuation of breastfeeding is suggested. [50]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturates -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
retrieved_docs.content: Careful monitoring and individualized dosing are essential, and the decision-making process should consider the potential risks and benefits, considering the patient's age, weight, and overall health status. The treatment approach in pediatric RSE/SRSE involves a tailored, trial-and-error strategy due to the limited evidence and variability in individual responses. [53] Guidelines for severe pediatric TBI emphasize ICP control. In cases of refractory intracranial hypertension persisting after maximal medical and surgical interventions, the use of high-dose barbiturate therapy is recommended for hemodynamically stable patients. However, when implementing high-dose barbiturate therapy to address refractory intracranial hypertension, continuous monitoring of arterial blood pressure and providing cardiovascular support to uphold adequate cerebral perfusion pressure are imperative. This is crucial due to the common occurrence of cardiorespiratory instability in patients undergoing barbiturate coma. [54]

retrieved_docs.title: Barbiturate Toxicity -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Barbiturates are sedative-hypnotic drugs. They are derivates of barbituric acid and were introduced clinically in the early 1900s. Over the past 120 years, barbiturates have been used for a broad spectrum of indications, including insomnia, psychiatric disorders, anesthesia, alcohol withdrawal, seizures, and elevated intracranial pressure. [1] These drugs have a narrow therapeutic index, are highly addictive, and carry a high risk for toxicity. Thus, the use of these drugs has declined mainly in the last several decades in favor of agents with more favorable safety profiles. Despite a lower rate of barbiturate utilization overall, barbiturate toxicity is still a prevalent cause of significant morbidity and mortality that requires prompt and effective care to mitigate.

retrieved_docs.title: Pentobarbital -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Barbiturates are pregnancy category D drugs and have been shown to cause fetal damage if used in pregnant patients. This class of drugs can cross the placental barrier and distribute throughout the fetal tissue with the highest concentrations in the liver, brain, and placenta. Monitoring maternal blood levels for fetal safety in any pregnant patient taking these drugs is essential.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Pain Assessment And Management -- Issues of Concern -- Pain Management
retrieved_docs.content: Lorazepam [37] Kinetics/dynamics Pregnancy category: D Half-life: 8 to 20 hours Onset: IV: 1 to 5 minutes IM: 15 to 30 minutes Duration: 12 to 24 hours Dosing Adult Sedation IV: 0.02 to 0.06 mg/kg Seizure disorder / status epilepticus IV: 2 to 4 mg Pediatric Sedation IV: 0.025 to 0.05 mg/kg Seizure disorder / status epilepticus IV: 0.05 to 0.1 mg/kg every 10 to 15 minutes Safer to use in patients with impaired liver function than other benzodiazepines because it only requires hepatic glucuronidation into lorazepam-glucuronide. [36] Precautions Not recommended for MAI / RSI because of the time of onset but frequently used for long-term sedation following intubation. It requires propylene glycol as a diluent, and there are reports of propylene glycol toxicity associated with long-term infusions.

retrieved_docs.title: Pentobarbital -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Pentobarbital is not used widely in clinical medicine because of its poor safety profile, habituation, and lack of an antidote. All prescribing clinicians should be aware of the drug's toxicity and adverse event profile. Neurologists can use pentobarbital for refractory status epilepticus. [2] Withdrawal from pentobarbital can be life-threatening. Managing pentobarbital withdrawal requires a well-trained interprofessional healthcare team, including nurses, pharmacists, and several clinicians from different specialties. Without proper management, the morbidity and mortality from unrecognized pentobarbital withdrawal are high.

retrieved_docs.title: Theophylline Toxicity -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Seizures: In adults, benzodiazepines (lorazepam, midazolam, diazepam) are the first-line treatment for theophylline-induced seizures.  Phenobarbital and continuous infusion of propofol or midazolam can be used for seizures refractory to benzodiazepines. In pediatrics, benzodiazepines are the first-line treatment for seizures. Phenobarbital or continuous infusion of midazolam or pentobarbital or propofol can be used for refractory seizures.

These agents are selected according to their distinct pharmacologic properties to manage seizures across diverse epilepsy syndromes. Enzyme‑mediated interactions are critical: cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers modify the mechanisms of clobazam and topiramat and are contraindicated with flibanserin (a benzimidazoly‑derived compound), while P‑glycoprotein inhibitors alter the activity of flibanserin and zonisamid. Awareness of CYP3A4‑ and P‑glycoprotein‑mediated drug interactions is essential for optimizing antiepileptic regimens, minimizing adverse effects, and improving patient outcomes. Valproat (valproic acid) poisoning can cause severe neurological injury and present with generalized seizures classified as نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة, originating in the cerebrum (velký mozek). Mefobarbital is an effective therapeutic option for controlling these seizures. Hypoxic conditions (عَوَز الأكسجين, oxygen

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 179
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (EPC) comprises a large repertoire of drugs used to manage and prevent seizures in patients with epilepsy (الصرع). Representative agents include lamotrigine, tiagabine and tiagabine hydrochloride, acetazolamide sodium, ethotoin, phenytoin sodium, mephenytoin, valproate calcium (valproát vápenatý), magnesium valproate, zonisamide, ethosuximide, felbamate, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, and additional agents such as fenytoin, mefenytoin, and lakosamid. These medications act through diverse mechanisms—stabilizing neuronal membranes, enhancing inhibitory neurotransmission, or modulating ion channels—to reduce seizure frequency and severity. Epilepsy is associated with serious acute complications, including coma, stupor, and brain damage, which may necessitate respiratory assistance (oxygen support) to prevent further neurological deterioration. A specific subtype, benign neonatal epilepsy, shares the underlying pathophysiology of epilepsy but typically carries a more favorable prognosis. In clinical practice, antiepileptic drugs are often part of polypharmacy regimens. Dextropropoxyfen is frequently co‑administered with fenytoin, and fenytoin is commonly combined with oxycodone to address both seizure control and analgesia. Recognizing these drug‑drug and drug‑condition relationships enables clinicians to tailor comprehensive treatment plans that optimize seizure management, mitigate severe neurological outcomes, and improve long‑term patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: as an anticonvulsant), and highlights safety considerations such as hypersensitivity contraindications and teratogenic risks, thereby supporting accurate drug selection and optimized patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: The seizure type نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة is classified within the broader category of epilepsy; convulsions, indicating that it represents a generalized onset non‑motor seizure. In clinical practice, the barbiturate mefobarbital may be used to treat نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة, providing therapeutic benefit for patients who experience this specific form of seizure. Recognizing نوبة بداية عدم الحركة المعممة as part of epilepsy; convulsions informs appropriate selection of mefobarbital in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2493
retrieved_docs.content: Mefobarbital may be used to treat generalized onset seizures, offering therapeutic benefit in controlling these seizure episodes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 244
retrieved_docs.content: The antiepileptic therapeutic class (EPC) comprises a broad spectrum of agents used to treat الصرع (epilepsy), including subtypes such as benign neonatal epilepsy, and aims to prevent severe neurological sequelae such as coma, stupor, and brain damage. Core EPC members include valproate (and its calcium, sodium, magnesium salts and related derivatives), lamotrigine, tiagabine (including tiagabine hydrochloride), ethotoin, phenytoin (phenytoin sodium, mephenytoin), ethosuximide, felbamate, fosphenytoin (fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, topiramate, vigabatrin, zonisamide, and others. These drugs exert anticonvulsant effects through distinct mechanisms, allowing clinicians to tailor seizure control to individual patients and specific seizure types. Polypharmacy considerations are highlighted by observed co‑occurrence patterns: dextropropoxyfen frequently co‑occurs with phenytoin, and phenytoin often co‑occurs with the opioid analgesic oxycodone, underscoring the need for vigilance regarding drug‑drug interactions. Oxygen deprivation (hypoxia) is closely associated with the same critical outcomes—coma, stupor, and irreversible brain injury—because insufficient cerebral oxygen supply can precipitate a spectrum of unconscious states and permanent neuronal damage. Recognizing the interrelationship between epilepsy, its antiepileptic treatments, hypoxic events, and the potential for severe neurological impairment is essential for individualized therapy, careful monitoring of concomitant medications, and timely intervention to prevent lasting neurological dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 2456
retrieved_docs.content: Epilepsy (الصرع) is a neurological disorder that can present in various forms, including benign neonatal epilepsy (epilepsie benigní neonatální), which is classified as a subtype of epilepsy. A broad spectrum of antiepileptic medications is recognized as potential treatments for epilepsy. Valproic acid derivatives such as valproát vápenatý, Valproat, and kyselina valproová, as well as the related compound felbamát, are commonly employed to control seizures. Additional agents with efficacy in epilepsy include gabapentin enacarbil, lamotrigin, levetiracetam, mefenytoin, phenytoin sodium, tiagabin, trimethadion, and zonisamid. Each of these drugs may be used to reduce seizure frequency and improve patient outcomes across the spectrum of epileptic disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 4 -- Community 2938
retrieved_docs.content: of these compounds enables clinicians to perform comprehensive risk assessments, optimize dosing, implement safety measures, and conduct vigilant monitoring to minimize harm while achieving therapeutic objectives.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 911
retrieved_docs.content: Poisoning caused by valproate is a toxic condition in which the medication valproate acts as the causative agent.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2265
retrieved_docs.content: Benign neonatal epilepsy is classified as a subtype of epilepsy, indicating that it shares the fundamental pathophysiological features of the broader seizure disorder. A wide range of antiepileptic medications may be employed to manage epilepsy, reflecting the diversity of therapeutic options available for different seizure types and patient needs. Felbamate, gabapentin enacarbil, lamotrigine, magnesium valproate, mefenytoin, methsuximide, phenytoin sodium, the sodium salt of valproic acid (sodná sůl kyselí valproové), tiagabin (tiagabine), trimethadion, and zonisamid (zonisamide) are all agents that may be used to treat epilepsy. The inclusion of both older drugs such as phenytoin sodium and newer formulations like gabapentin enacarbil underscores the evolving landscape of epilepsy management, where clinicians select appropriate therapy based on seizure type, patient age, comorbidities, and drug safety profiles. Effective use of these medications is essential for controlling seizures, improving quality of life, and reducing the long‑term neurological impact of epilepsy, including its benign neonatal form.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 1 -- Community 28
retrieved_docs.content: (topiramat), and many others. These agents are grouped by mechanism: sodium‑channel blockers (phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin sodium, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, zonisamide, lakosamid); GABA‑enhancers (vigabatrin, tiagabine, tiagabine hydrochloride, gabapentin enacarbil); calcium‑channel modulators (lamotrigine, topiramate); and additional pathways targeted by valproic‑acid derivatives, methsuximide, trimethadion, mefenytoin, and primidon. This mechanistic taxonomy enables clinicians to tailor therapy to seizure type, patient comorbidities, and tolerability, thereby optimizing seizure control. Cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers modify the pharmacodynamics of the antiepileptics klobazam and topiramate, necessitating dose adjustments or therapeutic drug monitoring to avoid clinically significant interactions. In practice, phenytoin is frequently co‑prescribed with the analgesic dextropropoxyfen and the opioid oxycodone, a polypharmacy pattern that raises concerns about combined toxicity,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 166
retrieved_docs.content: Additional antiepileptics are related to the class through an “isa” relationship, underscoring their shared pharmacological category. Zonisamide achieves its therapeutic effect by inhibiting the P‑glycoprotein transporter, classifying it as a P‑glycoprotein inhibitor. The metabolic activity of cytochrome P450 3A4 inducers influences the mechanism of action and pharmacokinetics of both klobazam and topiramate, highlighting the need to consider enzyme‑inducing drug interactions when selecting antiepileptic therapy. Generalized onset seizures (cerebral convulsions) are appropriately treated with mefobarbital. Valproate poisoning, a toxic condition, is caused by the agent Valparat (valproate). Pharmacovigilance data show that the non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory drug dextropropoxyfen co‑occurs with the antiepileptic fenytoin, and fenytoin co‑occurs with the opioid analgesic oxykodon, indicating potential polypharmacy interactions among an NSAID, an antiepileptic and an opioid. Epilepsy is

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 375
retrieved_docs.content: Antiepileptic agents constitute a therapeutic class indicated for the treatment of epilepsy and its subtypes, such as benign neonatal epilepsy. This class encompasses a large number of individual drugs, including valproate (calcium valproate and magnesium valproate), ethosuximide, ethotoin, felbamate, fosphenytoin (and fosphenytoin sodium), gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, lamotrigine, levetiracetam, mephenytoin, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin sodium, primidon, tiagabine (tiagabin, tiagabin hydrochloride), trimethadion, and vigabatrin. All of these agents share the common mechanism of modulating neuronal excitability to prevent seizure activity. Recognizing the hierarchical relationship—where the broad antiepileptic class contains these specific agents—allows clinicians to select therapy based on seizure type, patient comorbidities, tolerability, and drug‑specific properties, thereby optimizing patient care in epilepsy management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2679
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class of antiepileptic agents encompasses a broad range of medications used to prevent and control seizures. Within this class, lamotrigin is identified as a specific child agent, indicating its role as a distinct member of the antiepileptic group. Additional agents classified under antiepileptics include mefenytoin, sodium valproate (valproát sodný), calcium valproate (valproát vápenatý), vigabatrin, zonisamid, felbamát, fosphenytoin sodium, gabapentin enacarbil, magnesium valproate, phenytoin sodium, tiagabin hydrochloride, and trimethadion. Each of these compounds shares the common therapeutic purpose of stabilizing neuronal activity to reduce the frequency and severity of epileptic events, and together they represent the diverse pharmacologic options available for individualized seizure management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 2142
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC] is classified within the therapeutic class of antiepileptics, a broad pharmacologic group used to prevent or control seizures. The antiepileptics class includes numerous individual agents, each sharing the core purpose of seizure management. Among these agents are ethotoin, mefenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium, gabapentin enacarbil, lakosamid, levetiracetam, methsuximide, primidon, tiagabin, tiagabin hydrochlorid, and trimethadion. Within the hierarchical structure of this class, tiagabin is identified as a child subclass of antiepileptics, reflecting its specific pharmacologic profile while still belonging to the overall antiepileptic therapeutic category. Together, these drugs represent the diverse options available for clinicians to tailor antiepileptic therapy to individual patient needs, optimizing seizure control and minimizing adverse effects.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 1 -- Community 54
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic class “Anti‑epileptic Agent [EPC]” (anticonvulsants) is organized hierarchically to encompass a broad spectrum of agents used for the treatment of epilepsy (الصرع). Core members include Valproat (valproic acid and its salts – sodium valproate, magnesium valproate, calcium valproate) together with the child concepts lamotrigine, tiagabine (and tiagabine hydrochloride), and the subclass “Other antiepileptics in ATC”. Additional antiepileptic drugs listed in the class are ethotoin, phenytoin (phenytoin sodium, fosphenytoin, fosphenytoin sodium), mephenytoin, vigabatrin, zonisamide, ethosuximide, felbamate, gabapentin enacarbil, lacosamide, levetiracetam, methsuximide, oxcarbazepine, primidone, trimethadione, topiramate, and clobazam. Benign neonatal epilepsy is defined as a subtype of epilepsy, and epilepsy itself is included in the systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ) grouping, reflecting its relevance to autoimmune disease classifications. Several agents—particularly clobazam and topiramate—induce the cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme system, and many antiepileptics are metabolized by this pathway. Valproate‑related toxicity is directly attributable to valproate compounds, with Valproat identified as the causative agent in valproate poisoning. In adverse‑event reporting, dextropropoxyfen, phenytoin, and oxycodone are frequently mentioned together, suggesting common use in combination therapy or joint appearance in safety data. Proliferative glomerulonephritis (التهاب كبيبات الكلى التكاثري) is recognized as a renal manifestation of systemic lupus erythematosus (SMQ), underscoring its importance for diagnosis and management of patients with this autoimmune disease. This hierarchy and the pharmacologic, metabolic, safety, and disease‑association relationships among the listed agents support optimal patient management of epilepsy, its specific forms, and related systemic conditions.
